title: Chapter 10: The Kingdom, Named
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D3+D12 -->
A week passed.

A week in which Shadowfax got a new transmission (thanks to a GoFundMe started by a stranger whose Instagram bio said "cried for three hours after watching the strawberry farm video"). A week in which Chloe did three beachside "consultations" — free, of course — for people who had seen her on TikTok and driven hours to find her. A week in which Sam's spreadsheet grew two new columns: "Souls Liberated" (7) and "Probable Arrest Warrants" (0, somehow).

They stayed at the Desert Rose Inn because Gus the tow truck driver knew the owner and got them a weekly rate, and because the old man at the counter had started greeting them with "Your Majesty" and meaning it.

On the seventh morning, Chloe sat on the Santa Monica Pier at sunrise. The crown was on the bench beside her. She was eating a breakfast burrito from a stand run by a man named Hector who had seen the TikTok and given her the burrito for free. "You're doing the Lord's work," Hector had said. "Also, the video where you called that guy a goblin made my wife laugh so hard she almost went into labor."
Sam arrived with two coffees and the spreadsheet.
"Morning report," Sam said, sitting down. "Total views: 12 million. Total souls liberated: 7. Total bridges burned: approximately all of them. Total regrets: zero."
Chloe nodded, chewing her burrito. "Acceptable metrics."

"More than acceptable. You've achieved in one week what most people don't achieve in a lifetime: you've made seven strangers rethink their entire lives, you've made a tow truck driver's wife call her mother, and you've made me believe — against all evidence and my own personality — that maybe quitting my job to drive a dying Honda to Los Angeles was not, in fact, the worst decision I've ever made."
"That's the spreadsheet talking?"
"That's me talking."
Chloe stopped chewing. She looked at Sam. Sam was looking at the ocean, not at Chloe, which was how Sam delivered her most honest statements — aimed at something that couldn't look back.
"You've changed," Chloe said.
"You said that's how you know you're changing. When you don't know yet."
"Do you know yet?"
"I know something. I know I'm happier in a motel with broken AC and a friend in a plastic crown than I ever was in a corner office with a 401(k)."
Chloe set down her burrito. "That's the nicest thing anyone has ever said to me."
"You said that yesterday."
"It keeps being true."
A seagull landed on the pier railing and stared at Chloe's burrito. Chloe stared back.
"This is a good place to pause," Chloe said to the empty air beside her.
Sam looked up from the spreadsheet. "Who are you talking to?"
"The readers. Ten chapters. Good arc. Satisfying volume conclusion. They've earned a rest."
"There's no one there."
"There are thousands of people there. They just finished a book and they're deciding whether to leave a five-star review."

Sam considered this. Then she turned to the empty space beside her and said: "If you're still here, thank you. She's exhausting. But she's also right about basically everything. Which is the most exhausting part."
"That's a good closing line," Chloe said.
"Is it?"
"Volume one, concluded. Volume two: the kingdom expands. There are more windmills. More dark wizards. More souls to liberate."
"More gas stations?"
"Many more gas stations."
Sam closed the spreadsheet. "I'll update the budget."

Below them, the Pacific kept doing what the Pacific does. The pier creaked. The seagull got bored and flew away. Chloe put her crown back on — not because she needed it, but because it was hers, and she'd earned it, and it was no longer a prop.
"Ready?" Sam asked.
"For what?"
"Whatever's next."
Chloe stood up. She looked at the ocean, at the city, at the spreadsheet in Sam's hand, at the Honda with its new transmission waiting in the motel parking lot.
"I was born ready," Chloe said. "I just didn't know what for."
Sam didn't correct her. She just started walking toward the car.
And Chloe followed, crown catching the morning light, poncho billowing in the ocean breeze, sword at her hip, heading toward whatever came next.
*End of Volume One*
